title: Tracking Spending & Fixing a Blown Budget
summary: A budget only works if you check it against reality — and adjust without spiraling.
doThisToday: Open your banking app (or ask a parent to check theirs with you) and add up what you actually spent on wants this week.

cards.prompt: A budget you write once and never check again isn't really a budget.
cards.explanation: The plan is only half the job. The other half is tracking what you actually spend and comparing it to what you planned — that's how you catch problems early instead of at the end of the month.

cards.prompt: What's the easiest way for most teens to track spending?
options: Checking your banking app's transaction history weekly, or using a notes app to log purchases
Not tracking at all and hoping for the best
cards.explanation: Most banking apps already show you every transaction. A weekly 5-minute check-in is usually enough to catch overspending before it snowballs.

cards.prompt: You budgeted $90 for wants this month. By the 20th, you've already spent $110 on wants. By how much are you over budget so far?
cards.explanation: $110 − $90 = $20 over, with days still left in the month. Catching this now means you can adjust — catching it on day 30 means it's already too late to course-correct.

cards.prompt: True or false: blowing your budget one month means you're bad with money.
cards.explanation: Everyone overspends sometimes — the difference between people who are good with money and people who aren't isn't perfection, it's what happens next.

cards.prompt: You went $20 over on wants this month. What's the smartest next move?
options: Give up on budgeting since it clearly doesn't work
Pull $20 less from wants next month, or find $20 less in a non-essential need, to balance it out
cards.explanation: One small adjustment fixes it. Budgeting isn't about never messing up — it's about noticing and correcting course before a small miss becomes a big pattern.

cards.prompt: Which habit does the most to prevent a blown budget in the first place?
options: Checking your spending only once a month
Checking in on your spending weekly, so you can adjust mid-month instead of finding out too late
Not looking at your account until it's empty
cards.explanation: Weekly check-ins turn a month-end surprise into a small, easy mid-course correction. The frequency of checking matters more than the tool you use to do it.

cards.prompt: Sort each habit into Helps or Hurts your budget.

items.label: Not looking at your balance until it's empty
Logging purchases in a notes app
Forgetting about a small recurring subscription
Quitting your budget after one bad month

cards.explanation: Every "helps" habit is really just a way of catching problems earlier. Every "hurts" habit delays the moment you find out — which only makes the fix harder.

cards.prompt: Consistent tracking beats a perfect budget every time.
cards.explanation: A slightly-off budget you actually check every week will outperform a perfectly calculated budget you never look at again. Progress, not perfection.
